title: What are Java text blocks?
description: How do text blocks work in Java 15+?
options: Triple-quoted strings ("""...content...""") preserving formatting; auto-strip common leading whitespace. Support escape sequences, string interpolation with formatted(). Great for SQL, JSON, HTML literals
correct_answer: Triple-quoted strings ("""...content...""") preserving formatting; auto-strip common leading whitespace. Support escape sequences, string interpolation with formatted(). Great for SQL, JSON, HTML literals

title: What is the Java Memory Model?
options: Defines how threads interact through memory: happens-before relationships guarantee visibility of writes. Key rules: synchronized unlock → lock, volatile write → read, thread start → first action, final field semantics. Prevents data races
correct_answer: Defines how threads interact through memory: happens-before relationships guarantee visibility of writes. Key rules: synchronized unlock → lock, volatile write → read, thread start → first action, final field semantics. Prevents data races

title: Implement the Strategy pattern in Java.
What is Java's ClassLoader hierarchy?
options: Three-level hierarchy: Bootstrap (loads core JDK classes), Platform/Extension (loads platform APIs), Application (loads classpath). Uses delegation model — asks parent first. Custom ClassLoaders enable dynamic loading, hot-deploy, isolation
correct_answer: Three-level hierarchy: Bootstrap (loads core JDK classes), Platform/Extension (loads platform APIs), Application (loads classpath). Uses delegation model — asks parent first. Custom ClassLoaders enable dynamic loading, hot-deploy, isolation

title: What is the difference between wait/notify and Lock/Condition?
description: Compare Object wait/notify with ReentrantLock Condition.
options: wait/notify: tied to synchronized, single condition per lock, no timeout. Lock/Condition: explicit locking, multiple conditions per lock, tryLock with timeout, interruptible, fairness option. Lock/Condition is more flexible for complex synchronization
correct_answer: wait/notify: tied to synchronized, single condition per lock, no timeout. Lock/Condition: explicit locking, multiple conditions per lock, tryLock with timeout, interruptible, fairness option. Lock/Condition is more flexible for complex synchronization

description: Write a generic binary search method.

title: What are Java's atomic classes?
options: AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicReference provide lock-free thread-safe operations using CAS (Compare-And-Swap). Methods: get(), set(), compareAndSet(), incrementAndGet(), getAndUpdate(). More efficient than synchronized for simple operations
correct_answer: AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicReference provide lock-free thread-safe operations using CAS (Compare-And-Swap). Methods: get(), set(), compareAndSet(), incrementAndGet(), getAndUpdate(). More efficient than synchronized for simple operations

title: What is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy in Java?
description: How to clone objects in Java?
options: Shallow copy copies field values (references still point to same objects); deep copy recursively copies all referenced objects. Implement via clone() with Cloneable, copy constructors, or serialization. Copy constructors are generally preferred
correct_answer: Shallow copy copies field values (references still point to same objects); deep copy recursively copies all referenced objects. Implement via clone() with Cloneable, copy constructors, or serialization. Copy constructors are generally preferred

title: Implement a Java BlockingQueue from scratch.
What is the difference between Runnable and Callable?
options: Runnable: run() returns void, cannot throw checked exceptions. Callable<V>: call() returns a value of type V, can throw checked exceptions. Callable used with ExecutorService.submit() returning Future<V> to get result
correct_answer: Runnable: run() returns void, cannot throw checked exceptions. Callable<V>: call() returns a value of type V, can throw checked exceptions. Callable used with ExecutorService.submit() returning Future<V> to get result

title: What is Java's reflection API?
description: How does reflection work in Java?
options: java.lang.reflect allows inspecting/modifying classes, methods, fields at runtime; Class.forName(), getMethod(), invoke(), setAccessible(true). Used by frameworks (Spring, Hibernate), serialization, testing. Performance overhead and breaks encapsulation
correct_answer: java.lang.reflect allows inspecting/modifying classes, methods, fields at runtime; Class.forName(), getMethod(), invoke(), setAccessible(true). Used by frameworks (Spring, Hibernate), serialization, testing. Performance overhead and breaks encapsulation

title: Implement a Java ReadWriteLock example.
What are Java's functional interfaces?
description: What are the main built-in functional interfaces?
options: Interfaces with exactly one abstract method: Function<T,R> (T→R), Predicate<T> (T→boolean), Consumer<T> (T→void), Supplier<T> (→T), BiFunction<T,U,R>, UnaryOperator<T>, BinaryOperator<T>. Enable lambda expressions
correct_answer: Interfaces with exactly one abstract method: Function<T,R> (T→R), Predicate<T> (T→boolean), Consumer<T> (T→void), Supplier<T> (→T), BiFunction<T,U,R>, UnaryOperator<T>, BinaryOperator<T>. Enable lambda expressions

title: What is the difference between TreeMap and HashMap?
options: HashMap: O(1) avg operations, no ordering, allows one null key. TreeMap: O(log n) operations, sorted by keys (natural or Comparator), no null keys, implements NavigableMap with floorKey(), ceilingKey(), subMap()
correct_answer: HashMap: O(1) avg operations, no ordering, allows one null key. TreeMap: O(log n) operations, sorted by keys (natural or Comparator), no null keys, implements NavigableMap with floorKey(), ceilingKey(), subMap()
